claims.claim: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions causes emissions of toxic gases hydrogen fluoride (HF) and phosphoryl fluoride (POF3), where POF3 can be considered a precursor of HF in humid environments.
claims.quote: Decomposition of LiPF6-containing electrolytes under thermal heating conditions has been found to cause emissions of toxic gases such as hydrogen ﬂuoride, HF, and phosphoryl ﬂuoride, POF3, which, in humid environments, can be considered a precursor of HF

claims.claim: Non-cyclic carbonates (DMC, DEC, EA) evaporate at temperatures below 100°C, while cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100°C.
claims.quote: the non-cyclic carbonates are more volatile and will evaporate at temperatures below 100 (cid:3)C, while the cyclic carbonates (EC and PC) have negligible vapor pressure at low temperature and evaporate at temperatures above 100 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: Emissions of HF and POF3 were simultaneously detected from 100°C to 170°C during thermal heating of extracted Li-ion battery electrolyte containing LiPF6.
claims.quote: Regarding the decomposition of LiPF6, emissions of HF and one of its precursors, POF3, have been distinctly and almost simultaneously detected in the TG/FT-IR analysis from 100 (cid:3)C to 170 (cid:3)C

claims.claim: The simultaneous appearance and decrease of HF and POF3 emissions following a similar pattern may be explained by insufficient residual humidity to convert POF3 into HF.
claims.quote: The fact that the emissions of both HF and POF3 simultaneously appear around 100 (cid:3)C and decrease at 170 (cid:3)C following a similar pattern might be explained by the fact that the residual amount of humidity was insufﬁcient to convert POF3 in HF

claims.claim: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed during analysis because PF5 is likely too short-lived due to its instability and tendency to react with residual water or organic solvents.
claims.quote: No vibrational bands for PF5 were observed in the analysis, as it probably is too short-lived because of its instability and tendency to react with residual water or with the organic solvents

claims.claim: For about half of the working cells, for all levels of cycle ageing (0–300 cycles), ignition of accumulated battery-released gases occurred approximately 15 seconds after the thermal runaway, resulting in a gas explosion.
claims.quote: For about half of the working cells, for all levels of cycle ageing, ignition of the accumulated battery released gases occurred about 15 s after the thermal runaway resulting in a gas explosion.

claims.claim: Dead cells underwent thermal runaway but showed significantly higher thermal runaway temperatures, lower temperature rise rates, lower peak temperatures, and did not emit sparks, flames, fire, or cause gas explosions, compared to working cells.
claims.quote: The dead cells have a signiﬁcantly higher thermal runaway temperature, lower temperature rise rate and lower peak temperatures... the video analysis did not show any ﬂares/sparks/jet ﬂames, nor ﬁre or gas explosion occurred.

claims.claim: Toxic gas emissions of CO, HF, and POF3 were detected only in the third vent (simultaneous with thermal runaway), regardless of whether fire occurred or not, and were not detected in the first or second vents.
claims.quote: Gas emissions of CO, HF and POF3 were measured in all four tests where the FTIR gas measurement was employed, regardless if there was a ﬁre or not... HF and POF3 are not yet detected. The large cell opening of the cell safety vent occurs in the third vent, and this is the only vent phase where HF as well as POF3 are detected.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature for working cells was approximately 190°C and showed a weak local minimum between 100 and 200 cycles within the tested range of 0–300 cycles, while dead cells had thermal runaway temperatures between 201 and 205°C.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway temperature was about 190 °C and was shown to have a weak correlation with the number of cycles in the ageing process, having a minimum value between 100 and 200 cycles, within the tested cycle range of 0–300 cycles... the thermal runaway temperature values for the working cells are about 190 °C, and range between 201 and 205 °C for the dead cells.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature for the same cell type differed by approximately 50°C between oven external heating tests (approximately 190°C) and ARC test measurements (approximately 140°C), demonstrating that thermal runaway temperature values depend on the test method and its definition.
claims.quote: If the thermal runaway temperature is determined from the ARC measurement in a similar way as used in the oven experiment, the result is approximately 140 °C... The results for the two measurements thus diﬀer by about 50 °C. The thermal runaway temperature values for the same type of cell thus depend on the test method and on its deﬁnition.

claims.claim: During overcharge tests of a commercial LFP cylindrical 26650 cell at 2C rate, venting and thermal runaway were observed without explosion in seven out of nine reproducible tests, while in the other two tests the cell surface temperature rose to 300°C and 350°C respectively, leading to explosion.
claims.quote: During the seven reproducible overcharge tests, venting and thermal runaway were observed without explosion. During the other two, the cell surface temperature rose respectively to 300 °C and 350 °C, possibly caused by a strong internal short circuit, leading to an explosion that precluded gas analysis.

claims.claim: The first gas release from the LFP cell during overcharge occurred at a surface temperature of 53°C and a SOC of 120%, with detected species including DMC, EMC, CH4, CO, CH3OCHO, CH3OCH3, and CO2.
claims.quote: At 362s and as mentioned previously in section 3.1. (step D in Fig. 3b), a small gaseous production is observed. This occurs at a surface temperature of 53 °C and for a SOC of 120%. The detected molecules are: DMC, EMC, CH4, CO, CH3OCHO, CH3OCH3 and CO2.

claims.claim: HF formation during overcharge testing appeared only when all other gas species concentrations began to decrease, indicating that HF formation is decoupled from the other reaction mechanisms.
claims.quote: The HF formation mechanism appears to be decoupled from the other reaction mechanisms as HF appears when all the other gases start to decrease.

claims.claim: The amount of CO2 present in the vent gases released during overcharge of the LFP cell is not sufficient to prevent flammability, likely counterbalanced by the presence of flammable gases such as H2.
claims.quote: we showed that the amount of CO2 in the vent gases is thus not suﬃcient to prevent ﬂammability and is probably counterbalanced by the presence of ﬂammable gases such as H2.

claims.claim: The similarity in vent gas composition between overcharge abuse tests and thermal abuse tests on an overcharged LFP cell indicates that temperature, rather than oxidoreduction reactions, is the key parameter responsible for the main off-gas release.
claims.quote: The similarity in the vent gas composition obtained either in overcharge abuse tests or in thermal abuse tests on an overcharged cell indicates that oxidoreduction reactions seem to be poorly responsible for the main oﬀ-gas release by the cell whereas temperature is the key parameter.

claims.claim: Seven gas species (CO2, CO, H2, C2H4, CH4, C2H6, and C3H6) make up more than 99 vol.-% of the total venting gas during lithium-ion cell thermal runaway.
claims.quote: These seven components make up more than 99 vol.-% of the total gas.

claims.claim: The gas composition of venting gas does not depend on the total amount of generated venting gas volume.
claims.quote: There is no dependency of the gas composition on the total amount of generated venting gas visible. The different concentrations are stable over all conducted tests.

claims.claim: At low venting gas volumes, most combustible venting gas is oxidized to CO2, but with increasing venting gas volume the limited amount of O2 restricts this reaction and CO2 concentration drops to a stable value.
claims.quote: At low venting gas volumes most of the combustible venting gas is being reduced to CO2, but with increasing volume of venting gas the limited amount of O2 restricts this reaction and the CO2 concentration drops to a stable value.

claims.claim: Gas concentration measurements with venting gas volumes below 50 liters cannot be evaluated due to the influence of available O2 from ambient air in the autoclave.
claims.quote: Therefore, the influence of available O2 is considered negligible for venting gas volumes Vvent > 50 l. Hence, gas concentrations measurements with venting gas volumes Vvent < 40 l can not be evaluated.

claims.claim: Higher volumetric energy density leads to a lower thermal runaway onset temperature in lithium-ion cells, with an average slope of approximately -0.42 K l Wh^-1.
claims.quote: a higher energy density leads to a lower thermal runaway onset Temperature Tonset within lithium-ion cells... The average slope of this band is konsetDens ≈ −0.42 K l Wh−1.

claims.claim: Hard case cells eject a higher proportion of solid mass relative to total mass loss during thermal runaway compared to pouch cells, due to overpressure valves concentrating escaping gas through a small hole producing higher stream velocities.
claims.quote: The shallower slope for hard case cells means a higher portion of solid mass in the total mass loss compared to pouch cells. This can be explained by the over pressure valves usually found in hard case cell cans. These valves concentrate the escaping venting gas through a small hole in the housing and therefore produce higher stream velocities, with more power to rip off pieces from the electrodes and eject them.

claims.claim: The average venting gas to capacity proportion is 1.96 l Ah^-1, which lies within the literature range of 1.3 to 2.5 l Ah^-1 for regular electrolytes.
claims.quote: It illustrates an average gas to capacity proportion of kgasCap = 1.96 l Ah−1. This proportion lays well within the values mentioned in literature, ranging in 1.3 l Ah−1 ≤ kgasCapLit1 ≤ 2.5 l Ah−1 for regular electrolytes.

claims.claim: As SOC increases, the fraction of hydrogen and carbon-monoxide in lithium-ion cell vent gas increases while carbon-dioxide decreases, for LCO, LFP, and NCA chemistries.
claims.quote: As the SOC increases, the fraction of hydrogen and carbon-monoxide increases while the carbon-dioxide decreases for LCO, LFP, and NCA chemistries.

claims.claim: For LCO cells, below 40% SOC flammable gases make up less than 25% of the vent gas volume, with the remainder being carbon-dioxide; at 40% SOC there is a sudden jump in flammable gas volume and a decrease in carbon-dioxide volume.
claims.quote: In the FAA study [26] of LCO cells, below 40% SOC flammable gases make up less than 25% of the volume, with the remainder being carbon-dioxide. At 40% SOC, there is a sudden jump in the flammable gas volume and a decrease in the carbon-dioxide volume.

claims.claim: For LFP cells, total flammable gas production is below 20% of volume fraction at 25% SOC, but above 30% by volume at 50% SOC and increases further as SOC increases.
claims.quote: The LFP cells have a similar trend at 25% SOC, as the total flammable gas production is below 20% of the volume fraction. At 50% SOC, the flammable gas production is above 30% by volume and increases further as the SOC increases.

claims.claim: NCA cells have the highest peak laminar flame speed (0.69 to 1.07 m/s at 100% SOC) compared to LCO (0.50 to 0.66 m/s), NMC (0.51 to 0.66 m/s), and LFP (0.37 to 0.42 m/s) at 100% SOC, due to higher hydrogen concentration and lower carbon-dioxide in NCA vent gas.
claims.quote: the NCA cells have the highest overall flame speed between 0.6 and 0.8 for the peaks at an equivalence ratio of 1.2–1.4. This can directly be linked to an increased amount of hydrogen and much less carbon-dioxide in the vent gas compositions for the NCA batteries.

claims.claim: The CAFT method has 26%–44% error for battery vent gas LFL prediction due to hydrogen having a lower adiabatic flame temperature than hydrocarbons, while Jones and Bounaceur methods show less than 5% error.
claims.quote: the error using the CAFT method is 26%–44%. The method by Bounaceur using the adiabatic mixing of the fuels gives results very similar to Jones. The error with this method is just slightly higher than Jones

claims.claim: At the separator collapsing temperature Tsc, the partial pressure of gas generated by redox reactions (Pg) is nearly 3922.9 kPa, which is over six times larger than the electrolyte vapor pressure (PELE).
claims.quote: At the temperature point of Tsc, Pg is nearly 3922.9 kPa, which is over six times larger than PELE.

claims.claim: At the venting temperature of 405.5 K for an intact 100% SOC NCM 811 battery cell, Pg is 995.79 kPa and PELE is 302.42 kPa, making the gas from redox reactions nearly three times larger than the electrolyte vapor pressure.
claims.quote: the venting temperature is 405.5 K with Pg and PELE corresponding to 995.79 kPa and 302.42 kPa respectively. The pressure of the gas generated by the redox reaction bears the main responsibility for the safety valve bursting, which is nearly three times larger than the vapor pressure of electrolyte.

claims.claim: The calculated total internal pressure inside the intact battery cell at venting is 1298.21 kPa, which lies within the manufacturer's specified pressure range of 1.3 MPa to 1.6 MPa.
claims.quote: The calculated pressure inside the battery cell is 1298.21 kPa totally, which lied in the reasonable pressure range provided by the manufacturer from 1.3 MPa to 1.6 MPa.

claims.claim: The relationship between temperature increasing rate and gas generation rate is not linear from Tonset to Tsc, but becomes approximately linear when temperature approaches Tsc, indicating a multi-step reaction process before Tsc.
claims.quote: the relationship between temperature increasing rate and gas generation rate is not linear until battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature... Since the rates of temperature increasing and gas generation are not linear, the reaction before the Tsc should be considered to be multi-steps. However, the relationship between both is approximately linear when the temperature approaches Tsc.

claims.claim: If the safety valve bursting pressure threshold is set too high, gas generated during thermal runaway inside the battery cell is not vented in time, resulting in commercial cell designs rupturing and exploding easily, especially at high temperatures.
claims.quote: if the threshold of the safety valve is set high, gas generation during thermal runaway inside the battery cells is not vented timely, resulting in commercial cell designs rupturing and exploding easily especially at high temperatures, presenting serious safety concerns.

claims.claim: During safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells, CH4 was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC, and CH4 concentration was positively correlated with SoC.
claims.quote: During safety venting, CH4 was consistently the most abundant gas detected across all SoC. At 25% SoC, the % volume increase observed for CH4 was 5.50%, compared to 0.15% CO2, 0.02% CO, 0.35% C2H6 and 0% H2. At 100% SoC, the % volume for CH4 was 6.8% compared to 0.29% CO2, 0.01% CO, 0.45% C2H6 and 0% H2.

claims.claim: No H2 was detected during safety venting of NMC-811 21700 cells across all tested SoC.
claims.quote: in our tests, no H2 was detected during safety venting. A similar observation was reported by J. Siegel, who noted that H2 is not detected consistently in first venting events.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, H2 peak % volume increases ranged from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC, showing a strong positive correlation between H2 concentration and SoC.
claims.quote: Our data revealed a strong correlation between H2 concentration and SoC, with peak % volume increases ranging from 0.20% at 5% SoC to 12.9% at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: Increasing SoC from 25% to 100% produced a 3 to 5-fold increase in total gas volume in both air and N2 atmospheres; in air, gas volume increased from 0.8 L to 3.9 L, and in N2 from 2.2 L to 6.5 L.
claims.quote: For both the air and N2 based tests, we observed a 3-5-fold jump in the total volume of gas produced when the SoC was increased from 25% to 100%. In air, the total gas generated increased from 0.8 L at 25% SoC to 3.9 L at 100% SoC; for the tests conducted in N2, 2.2 L of gas was produced at 25% SoC, which increased to 6.5 L at 100% SoC.

claims.claim: The onset gas detection window (duration between start of safety venting and start of thermal runaway) was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (142 s) compared to 5% SoC (262 s), and this window decreases with increasing SoC because threshold temperatures for safety venting and thermal runaway decrease with increasing SoC.
claims.quote: the window of opportunity to detect vent gas prior to thermal runaway was 120 s shorter at 100% SoC (t = 142 s) when compared to 5% SoC (t = 262 s) under the same test conditions. These windows are fundamentally influenced by the threshold temperature values for safety venting and thermal runaway, which decrease with increasing SoC. At 100% SoC, thermal runaway can initiate at a temperature up to 60 ◦C lower than at 5% SoC.

claims.claim: Fluorinated compounds in the amounts common in battery vent gases do not have any effects on ignition delay time or macroscopic combustion characteristics.
claims.quote: Comparing the ignition delay time for similar mixtures with (Case 2) and without (Case 3) fluorinated compounds, it is clear that the presence of F does not affect the ignition.

claims.claim: Ignition of Li-ion battery vent gas mixtures is not sensitive to oxygen content and occurs at approximately the same ignition delay time in oxygen-depleted conditions as at high oxygen availability.
claims.quote: Note that ignition does not require a high oxygen content but occurs at about the same delay time in the oxygen-depleted case.

claims.claim: At temperatures below about 550°C, ignition of battery vent gas mixtures requires a long time or an external ignition source.
claims.quote: Ignition is highly temperature dependent and at temperatures below about 550 C ignition of the gas mixtures will require a long time or an external ignition source.

claims.claim: At 730°C, carbonate-containing vent gas mixtures ignite up to an order of magnitude slower than the most reactive H2/CO-rich mixtures.
claims.quote: it is clearly seen that the carbonate-containing mixtures (Case 1–3) ignite as much as an order of magnitude slower than the most reactive mixtures, among which Case 6 where the reactants consist of almost fifty per cent hydrogen gas ignite the fastest

claims.claim: The laminar burning velocity of Case 5 (SOC 100%) is about 50% higher than that of Case 4 (SOC 30%) for gases from the same NMC/LMO battery, demonstrating that combustion properties are highly dependent on SOC.
claims.quote: the laminar burning velocity for Case 5 (SOC 100%) is about 50% higher than that for Case 4 (SOC 30%), which clearly shows that the combustion properties of gases from a given battery are highly dependent on the SOC during the thermal event leading up to the gas production.

claims.claim: A partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (bag volumes of 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (bag volume of 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject a two-car garage door.
claims.quote: a partial volume deflagration with between 124 L and 310 L of NCA synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 330 L and 826 L), or approximately 91 L of LFP synTREG (𝑉𝑏𝑎𝑔 = 320 L), produced enough pressure to eject the two-car garage door.

claims.claim: The synTREG volumes sufficient to eject the garage door correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, whereas 25% of the well-mixed LFL is typically used when assessing explosion hazard precipitation.
claims.quote: These synTREG volumes correspond to 1–2.5% of the enclosure volume LFL of the flammable gas composition, where 25% of the well-mixed LFL is used when assessing the precipitation of an explosion hazard.

claims.claim: Significant structural damage including cracked wooden wall studs, dislodged drywall, deformed man-door latch plates, and ejected garage doors was observed at peak internal pressures of approximately 2.51–2.79 kPa, which are approximately 50% below the Jarrett and Baker thresholds for minor structural damage.
claims.quote: The 826 L NCA and 320 L LFP gas bag experiments achieved peak internal pressures of 2.79 kPa and 2.51 kPa, respectively. In both cases, the maximum pressure was approximately 50% less than the threshold established by Jarrett or Baker.

claims.claim: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas, which is attributed to the higher burning velocity of the LFP gas.
claims.quote: The LFP surrogate gas yielded greater maximum pressure and impulse per liter than the NCA surrogate gas. This is expected due to the higher burning velocity of the gas.

claims.claim: In each bag experiment case considered, the Mulpuru vented explosion model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise compared to experimental results, indicating it may provide conservative estimates for explosion hazard design calculations.
claims.quote: In each bag case considered, the model over-predicted the rate of pressure rise when compared to experimental results. This demonstrates that the Mulpuru model may provide more conservative estimates of the rate of pressure rise for explosion hazard design calculations.

claims.claim: For the 3302 L NCA gas bag experiment, the maximum overpressure was 4.12 kPa, which was lower than the 6.25 kPa measured in the 1651 L gas bag experiment, with the decrease attributed to early pressure relief through the garage door acting as a deflagration vent.
claims.quote: the largest tested bag volume of 3302 L deviates from the model trend, having a maximum overpressure of 4.12 kPa compared to 6.25 kPa for the 1651 L gas bag. This behavior may be attributed to the low-strength garage door, which relieved pressure and vented the still-combusting gas cloud, similar to a deflagration panel.

claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries (NMC811/SiC, 21700 and 18650 formats) reached venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370°C during thermal runaway, with surface temperatures surpassing 400°C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m/s.
claims.quote: Results indicate that the lithium-ion batteries reach venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370 °C, with surface temperatures surpassing 400 °C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m s−1.

claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 cathode, 18650 format) exhibited lower temperatures than lithium-ion batteries due to the absence of gas ignition during thermal runaway, with peak venting gas temperatures not exceeding 350°C and all surface temperatures remaining below 400°C.
claims.quote: Conversely, the investigated sodium-ion batteries exhibit lower temperatures due to the absence of gas ignition, precluding the estimation of venting gas velocity... the measured cell surface and venting gas temperatures remained under 400°C and no gas ignition was observed in the tested cells.

claims.claim: Peak mass flow rates during thermal runaway reached 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, but lasted only several milliseconds, while average mass flow rates ranged between 30 g/s and 300 g/s.
claims.quote: Peak mass flow rates for lithium-ion batteries reach 2000 g s−1, with average rates between 30 g s−1 and 300 g s−1... The peak values of the mass flow rate were even higher - 1500 g s−1 for 21 700 LIBs and 2000 g s−1 for 18 650 LIBs but lasting only several milliseconds.

claims.claim: Total mass loss during thermal runaway ranged from 55% for 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77% for 18650 lithium-ion batteries, with the lower mass loss in SIBs attributed to lower temperatures, absence of ignition, and lower energy content.
claims.quote: Total mass loss ranges from 55 % in 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77 % in 18650 lithium-ion batteries... The investigated SIBs have a lower nominal capacity and, consequently, lower stored energy leading to less ejection during TR. No ignition of SIBs was observed in the high-speed videos, which also contributes to the lower mass loss of SIBs.

claims.claim: The TR start temperature of 18650 LIBs ranged between 190°C and 200°C, while the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs was on average 10°C to 15°C higher, indicating greater thermal stability of the investigated SIBs.
claims.quote: The TR start temperature of the 18 650 LIBs ranges between 190°C and 200°C determined based on the cell surface temperature 𝑇surface mid1, with some observed fluctuations between tests... Compared to LIBs, the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs is, on average, 10°C to 15°C higher, and, thus, the TR occurred later despite the same heating rate, indicating that investigated SIBs could be more thermally stable compared to investigated LIBs.

claims.claim: The venting gas velocity of LIBs in both 21700 and 18650 formats exceeded 100 m/s at the beginning of thermal runaway and exhibited exponential decay, falling below 50 m/s after 200 ms and below 25 m/s after 500 ms from the start of the optically visible TR event.
claims.quote: Both LIB formats show similar trends of venting gas velocity over time, exhibiting an exponential decay, as represented in Fig. 4. The high velocities exceeding 100 m s−1 at the beginning of TR can be attributed to the gas ignition. The venting gas velocity decreases after 200 ms, falling below 50 m s−1 in both LIB formats and further drops below the 25 m s−1 value after 500 ms since the beginning of the optically visible TR event.

claims.claim: A lower heating rate combined with a higher SOC leads to greater total mass loss of lithium-ion cells during thermal runaway, due to more homogeneous temperature distribution and simultaneous TR across all cell areas.
claims.quote: Willstrand et al. [6] demonstrated that the lower the heating rate and the higher the SOC level, the greater the total mass loss of the cell. The low heating rate is responsible for a homogeneous temperature distribution within the cell. Consequently, it may lead to simultaneous TR across all cell areas and higher mass loss.

claims.claim: The absence of gas ignition in investigated sodium-ion batteries may be primarily related to the cathode material (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2), which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of the investigated lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Therefore, the lack of gas ignition in investigated SIBs may be related primarily to the cathode material, which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of LIBs.

claims.claim: The mass flow rate for 18650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of gas ignition and the consequent unavailability of venting gas velocity values.
claims.quote: Notably, the mass flow rate for the 18 650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of ignition and the venting gas velocity values.

claims.claim: The transition time between safety valve opening and thermal runaway event under continuous 128 W heating was on average 128 s for 21700 LIBs, 115 s for 18650 LIBs, and 154 s for 18650 SIBs.
claims.quote: The transition time between the safety valve opening and the TR event with continuous heating at 128 W is, on average, 128 s for 21 700 LIBs, 115 s for 18 650 LIBs and 154 s for 18 650 SIBs.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway did not occur in NTM SIBs at any of the five SOCs tested (0%, 60%, 80%, 90%, 100%), whereas thermal runaway occurred in LFP LIBs at 60%, 80%, 90%, and 100% SOC but not at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rise rates (Rmax) of the NTM SIBs at five SOCs and the LFP LIB at 0 % SOC was not more than 1 ◦C/s during the heating, which indicated that the TR do not occur. However, the Rmax of the LFP LIBs at 60 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % SOCs was more than 1 ◦C/s and last for more than 3 s, meaning that the LFP LIBs at those SOCs experienced TR.

claims.claim: The safety valve opening temperature (Topen) of LFP LIBs was approximately 30% higher than that of NTM SIBs under the same SOC, indicating that the material inside the SIB was less thermally stable.
claims.quote: the values of Topen in the LFP LIBs were 120.4 ◦C, 118.9 ◦C, 119.0 ◦C, 116.5 ◦C, 120.0 ◦C for 0%–100% SOCs, respectively which was approximately 30 % higher than that of SIB under the same SOC. This further indicated that the material inside the SIB was less thermally stable.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (Tmax) and maximum temperature rise rate (Rmax) of LFP LIBs were 491.4°C and 48.9°C/s respectively, far exceeding those of NTM SIBs at 167.9°C and 0.6°C/s.
claims.quote: The Tmax and Rmax of the LFP LIBs were 491.4 ◦C and 48.9 ◦C/s, respectively, which were far more than that (167.9 ◦C and 0.6 ◦C/s) of the NTM SIBs

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the FED value for LFP LIBs (75.70186) was 28.90 times higher than that for NTM SIBs (2.53196), indicating significantly greater asphyxiation hazard from LFP LIB vent gases.
claims.quote: At 100 % SOC, the FED values of NTM SIBs and LFP LIBs were 2.53196 and 75.70186, respectively. The value for LFP LIBs is 28.90 times higher than that for NTM SIBs.

claims.claim: The maximum mass loss rate in LFP LIBs was approximately 2–7 times larger than that of NTM SIBs at the same SOC, with LFP LIB mass loss rates increasing from 11 g/s to 18 g/s as SOC increased from 0% to 100%.
claims.quote: the maximum mass loss rate in the LFP LIBs was about 2–7 times larger than that of the NTM SIBs at the same SOC, which was related to the gas release rate of the batteries. The maximum mass loss rates of the NTM SIBs were 4 g/s, 3 g/s, 2 g/s, 2 g/s, and 4 g/s while the that the LFP LIBs were 11 g/s, 13 g/s, 16 g/s, 17 g/s, and 18 g/s of from 0 % to 100 % SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: For NTM SIBs, CO is the first parameter to respond and is suitable as an early warning indicator of thermal hazard, whereas for LFP LIBs, voltage responds first and is suitable for early warning.
claims.quote: For the NTM SIB, the response order of the characteristic parameters is CO > H2 > voltage > CO2 > HF > surface temperature > EX. For the LFP LIB, the response order of characteristic parameters is voltage > surface temperature > HF > EX > CO2 > H2 > CO. Thus, for early warning parameters of thermal hazards, CO can be selected for NTM SIBs while voltage could serve for LFP LIBs.

claims.claim: The dominant gas emitted by NTM SIBs was CO2, whereas H2 accounted for the majority of released gases from LFP LIBs, with H2 in LFP LIBs reaching 5412 ppm at 100% SOC compared to a maximum of 92 ppm in NTM SIBs.
claims.quote: the dominant gas was CO2 for NTM SIB while H2 accounted for the majority of the released gases for LFP LIB... the maximum concentrations of H2 surged from 219 ppm to 5412 ppm as SOC increased, which was far larger than that for the NTM SIBs.

claims.claim: CO concentration in LFP LIBs at 60%, 80%, 90%, and 100% SOC exceeded the lethal threshold of 447 ppm, whereas CO concentrations in NTM SIBs at all SOCs (maximum 80 ppm at 100% SOC) remained significantly below the lethal threshold.
claims.quote: The maximum CO concentrations from 0 % to 100 % SOC were 48 ppm, 50 ppm, 51 ppm, 75 ppm, and 80 ppm, respectively, all of which were significantly lower than the lethal threshold... The maximum CO concentrations increased dramatically from 140 ppm to 3306 ppm, representing a 2261.43 % rise. The CO concentration at 60 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % SOCs were all significantly higher than the lethal threshold.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery, the primary ejected gases are H2 (55.22%), CO2 (23.17%), and CO (7.97%), with a single cell releasing an average of 6.6301 mol of gas.
claims.quote: the primary gases ejected during thermal runaway of 280Ah LiFePO4 batteries are H2 (55.22 %), CO2 (23.17 %), and CO (7.97 %), with a single cell releasing 6.6301 mol of gas

claims.claim: The mass ratio of non-condensable gas to condensable liquid (electrolyte) ejected during thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery is approximately 1:10, with ejected gas accounting for ~2% of total battery mass and condensed electrolyte liquid accounting for ~20%.
claims.quote: the mass of the ejected gas accounted for about 2 % of the total battery mass, the wreckage accounted for about 78.1 %, and the condensed electrolyte liquid accounted for about 19.9 %. This indicates that electrolyte condensation behavior cannot be ignored in energy storage systems. The flow of 1080.9 g electrolyte within the battery pack may induce arcing hazards. Furthermore, the mass ratio of gas to liquid in the ejected substances was approximately 1:10.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of two batteries in a battery pack, the pressure balance valve opened 36 times, with 72.2% of pressure relief events occurring at a rate of 0.0–0.5 kPa/s, and the maximum pressure reduction rate of 1.5–2.0 kPa/s occurring only once.
claims.quote: the pressure balance valve opened 36 times. The pressure threshold for the first opening differed from subsequent openings... Among these, the 0.0–0.5 kPa/s rate was the most frequent, accounting for 72.2 % of the total activations in this experiment.

claims.claim: The first opening of the pressure balance valve during battery pack thermal runaway occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at approximately 105.74–106.24 kPa, both slightly above the manufacturer-specified opening pressure of 104.825 kPa.
claims.quote: the first opening occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at around 105.74–106.24 kPa. The manufacturer-specified opening pressure for the pressure balance valve was 104.825 kPa, and the experimental results were slightly higher than this reference value.

claims.claim: The four corners inside the battery pack were identified as sites where thermal runaway airflow and electrolyte aerosols accumulated, evidenced by black ablation marks, requiring enhanced electrical insulation protection.
claims.quote: It is speculated that the TR airflow accumulated in the four corners, resulting in the formation of these black marks. Therefore, when designing battery modules, additional insulation protection should be provided for the batteries located at the edges and corners.

claims.claim: As battery capacity increases in LFP pouch cells under thermal abuse, the onset of thermal runaway is postponed, although the severity of the event becomes more pronounced.
claims.quote: our results indicate that, as the battery capacity increases, the onset of thermal runaway is postponed, although the severity of the event becomes more pronounced.

claims.claim: The 67 Ah LFP pouch cell emits approximately 21.60 ± 5e-3 L of gas during thermal runaway, markedly higher than the 6.22 ± 5e-3 L released by the 6 Ah cell.
claims.quote: The 67 Ah cell emits approximately 21.60 ± 5e-3 L of gas, markedly higher than the 6.22 ± 5e-3 L released by the 6 Ah cell.

claims.claim: Methane is identified as the first detectable gas during thermal runaway of LFP pouch cells, and TDLAS and NDIR sensors detect target gases 300 to 700 s before thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Methane is identified as the first detectable gas, TDLAS and non-dispersive infrared (NDIR) sensors exhibit superior response-ability, detecting the target gases 300 to 700 s before thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The gas production sequence for LFP pouch cells under thermal abuse follows CH4 first, then HF, then H2, with CO2 and CO detected last.
claims.quote: the sequence of gas detection with the commercial sensor module is CH4, followed by HF and H2, with CO2 and CO being detected last.

claims.claim: TDLAS cannot detect gas after thermal runaway because substantial smoke generated during thermal runaway obstructs the laser path and weakens light intensity to zero.
claims.quote: TDLAS can monitor CH4 generation in the early stage of TR of pouch batteries and detect weak gas leakage before the pouch ruptures. However, it is not suitable for detecting gas after TR. This is due to the substantial smoke generated during TR, which obstructs the laser path and weakens the light intensity.

claims.claim: The gas-to-capacity proportion for LFP pouch cells is approximately 0.54 L/Ah based on a linear fit to experimental data.
claims.quote: The resulting gas-to-capacity proportion, 𝐾𝑔𝑎𝑠 = 0.54 L/Ah, indicates that LFP pouch cells generate less gas compared to NCM cells, which have an average gas-to-capacity proportion closer to 0.4 L/Ah.

claims.claim: The 67 Ah LFP battery thermal runaway occurs 829 s later than the 6 Ah cell under 300 W heating, demonstrating that battery size influences the speed of thermal runaway onset.
claims.quote: Battery size influences the speed of TR, the 67 Ah battery has a more intense and rapid rise, with TR occurring 829 s later than the 6 Ah cell.

claims.claim: CH4 evolution from LFP battery anodes is primarily due to the reaction between the anode and electrolyte solvent, especially decomposition of dimethyl carbonate, when battery temperature exceeds 120°C.
claims.quote: When the battery temperature exceeds 120 ◦C, the evolution of CH4 is primarily due to the reaction between the anode and the electrolyte solvent, especially the decomposition of dimethyl carbonate (DMC).

claims.claim: H2 generation in LFP batteries is caused by the reaction between the binder and lithium in the battery anode when temperature exceeds 250°C, with PVdF and CMC reacting with lithium ions to produce H2.
claims.quote: H2 is generated by the reaction between the binder and lithium in the battery anode, which occurs when the temperature exceeds 250 ◦C. At this stage, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) react with lithium ions to produce H2.

claims.claim: In the 67 Ah LFP cell test, the TDLAS system detected CH4 concentration rising to 3.17 ppm before 930 s, approximately 300 s before the thermal runaway event, and the 67 Ah battery began generating significant smoke earlier than the 6 Ah cell.
claims.quote: The earliest response time of the TDLAS system is observed at 930 s. Comparing the temperature curves, it is evident that the 67 Ah battery begins generating significant smoke earlier, approximately 300 s before the TR event occurred.

claims.claim: The highest flame propagation velocity of LIB vent gases in a pipeline was about 20.4 m/s, occurring at approximately 12.5 ms during the spherical flame stage.
claims.quote: the flame velocity increased sharply to about 20.4 m/s in 12.5 ms. This corresponds to the spherical flame stage and the flame could propagate freely.

claims.claim: The highest overpressure of LIB vent gases flame propagation in a pipeline was about 0.595 MPa, which is lower than the overpressure of pure H2 or pure CH4 in air (about 0.7 MPa) due to the presence of inert gas in LIB vent gases.
claims.quote: The highest overpressure was about 0.595 MPa. This value was lower than the overpressure of pure H2 or pure CH4 in air (about 0.7 MPa), it was mainly because of the LIB vent gases including inert gas.

claims.claim: The maximum explosion overpressure inside the energy storage container reached 23.19 kPa at t = 149.6 ms, and the venting of pressure relief panels and escape door greatly decreased the overpressure.
claims.quote: At t = 149.6 ms, the maximum explosion overpressure reached 23.19 kPa inside the container... It is indicated that the pressure venting is necessary for fire safety.

claims.claim: In the energy storage container explosion scenario, the pressure relief panels opened when internal pressure reached 3 kPa, and the escape door opened when internal pressure reached 20 kPa.
claims.quote: At t = 0.0695 s, the pressure inside the box reached to 3 kPa, so the pressure relief panels opened... At t = 0.1417 s, the pressure inside the box reached 20 kPa, causing the door to open.

claims.claim: The maximum damage distance caused by explosion pressure of LIB vent gases in an energy storage container was about 10 m, using 4.8 kPa as the threshold for minimum damage, with the door side presenting higher risk than the pressure relief panel side.
claims.quote: Based on previous studies, 4.8 kPa was selected as the threshold for the minimum damage. The result showed that the overpressure of P8, P9 and P10 was close to 4.8 kPa, while the overpressure of P3, P4 and P5 was smaller than the threshold. It meant that the maximum distance of damage was about 10 m. It was also indicated that the risk of door side was higher than that of the relief panels side.

claims.claim: LFP cells have a higher failure temperature of approximately 250°C compared to NMC cells which fail at around 200°C under thermal abuse conditions.
claims.quote: It is apparent that LFP has a higher failure temperature of around 250°C compared to other chemistries. This indicates that LFP batteries have relatively better resilience to elevated heat stress. Failing temperature for LFP have has been reported to be around 200°C while that for the NMC is reported to be around 150°C

claims.claim: LFP cells smoke for more than 20 minutes under thermal abuse conditions, compared to approximately 5 minutes for NMC cells.
claims.quote: Compared to other cells, which smoked for around 5 min, the smoking duration for LFP exceeds 20 min

claims.claim: NMC1 and NMC3 produce the highest CO emissions, averaging over 150 L/kWh at 100% SOC, with peak emission rates surpassing 2 L/min, while LTO averages only 10–12 L/kWh.
claims.quote: NMC1 and NMC3 have the highest emission rate with more than 150 L/kWh at 100 % SOC, and peak emission rates surpassing 2 L/min... NMC1 averaging at 150 L/kWh while LTO averaging at 10–12 L/kWh

claims.claim: LFP cells emit up to 4 L/kWh of HF at 100% SOC, significantly more than NMC cells which range at 0.5–1.4 L/kWh.
claims.quote: LFP cells emit up to 4 L/kWh at 100 % SOC, which is significantly more than other chemistries. By comparison, the total emissions of NMC cells are lower, ranging at 0.5–1.4 L/kWh.

claims.claim: Battery chemistry influenced fire risk more than state of charge; NMC1 reaches a composite Risk Index of 4.2 at 75% SOC while LFP remains in the lower-risk band with RI approximately 2.3–3.3 across all SOC levels.
claims.quote: LFP stays consistently safe (RI ≈ 2.30–3.30) across the SOC window, reaffirming its superior thermal stability... NMC1 shows the steepest escalation, reaching RI = 4.20 at 75 % SOC and remaining high at full charge (RI = 3.99)

claims.claim: The SIB exhibited a thermal runaway trigger temperature of 58.15°C, while the LFP battery triggered thermal runaway at 196.65°C, demonstrating significantly lower thermal stability in the SIB.
claims.quote: The LFP battery reached TR at approximately 1659 s with a measured temperature of 208.13 °C, while the SIB exhibited earlier TR onset at 530 s with a notably lower surface temperature of 58.15 °C. The Ttr for LFP significantly exceeds that of SIB (LFP: 208.13 °C vs SIB: 58.15 °C), demonstrating superior thermal stability in the LFP system.

claims.claim: The time interval between safety valve opening and thermal runaway (ΔtSV-TR) was 12 s for SIB and 173 s for LFP, indicating significantly shorter emergency response time available for SIB.
claims.quote: The ΔtSV-TR was 12 s for SIB and 173 s for LFP. A comparison of these two values indicates that, under the same heating power conditions, the time available for fire prevention and evacuation is significantly shorter for SIB, thereby increasing the risks associated with TR.

claims.claim: The SIB's explosive limit range (ELR) after including EMC contribution was 32.5%, while the LFP's ELR was 21.21%, indicating greater combustion and explosion risk for SIB.
claims.quote: After considering the contribution of EMC, the explosive limit range of the SIB was reduced to 4.96 %–37.46 % with an ELR of 32.5 %, while that of the LFP battery decreased to 4.11 %–25.32 % with an ELR of 21.21 %.

claims.claim: The maximum mass loss rate per unit energy of SIB (0.33 g s⁻¹ Wh⁻¹) was approximately 6.6 times higher than that of LFP (0.05 g s⁻¹ Wh⁻¹) during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The maximum mass loss rate of SIB reached 172.6 g s−1, normalized to 0.33 g s−1 Wh−1, while that of LFP was only 25.6 g s−1, normalized to 0.05 g s−1 Wh−1.

claims.claim: The SIB produced approximately four times more total gas per unit energy than the LFP during thermal runaway (0.72 L Wh⁻¹ vs 0.18 L Wh⁻¹).
claims.quote: the total gas production is 0.72 L Wh−1 and 0.18 L Wh−1 for the SIB and LFP, respectively, indicating that the SIB produces approximately four times more gas than the LFP.

claims.claim: NMC cells exhibited earlier thermal runaway onset (173.6–215.1 °C), with higher-SOC cells initiating runaway at lower temperatures, whereas LFP cells required higher onset temperatures (241.9–249.2 °C) to trigger TR.
claims.quote: NMC cells exhibited earlier TR onset (173.6–215.1 ◦C), with higher-SOC cells initiating runaway at lower temperatures, whereas LFP cells required higher onset temperatures (241.9–249.2 ◦C) to trigger TR, reflecting greater thermal stability.

claims.claim: NMC cells at 100% SOC lost 57.7–61.4% of their mass during thermal runaway, while LFP cells showed consistently lower mass loss of approximately 11–13% with little variation between SOC levels.
claims.quote: NMC cells exhibited substantially higher mass loss, with 57.7–61.4 % of mass lost at 100 % SOC. In contrast, LFP cells showed consistently lower mass loss of ~11–13 %, with little variation between SOC levels.

claims.claim: Peak HF emissions exceeded 66,000 ppm during thermal runaway, as measured in the near-field vent plume of 18650 LFP cells at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: peak HF emissions exceeding 66,000 ppm

claims.claim: HF first appeared immediately after safety venting onset, providing a finite early-warning interval before thermal runaway; the venting window was approximately 1 minute for NMC and approximately 5 minutes for LFP under ~27 °C/min external heating.
claims.quote: HF becomes detectable immediately after safety venting, providing a finite warning interval before it decays and well before TR onset. In our tests, the venting window was ≈1 min for NMC and ≈5 min for LFP (Table 2), offering practical time for early detection, mitigation, and response.

claims.claim: Peak HF in LFP cells displayed a strong SOC dependence at both venting and TR stages, with the 50% SOC case producing approximately half the peak HF of the 100% SOC case.
claims.quote: peak HF in LFP displayed a strong SOC dependence at both venting and TR, with the 50 % SOC case approximately half of the 100 % SOC case.

claims.claim: Conventional gas analysis techniques using unheated or metallic transfer lines often underestimate or entirely lose HF due to its highly adsorptive and corrosive nature, causing existing safety models to be based on HF levels orders of magnitude lower than true concentrations.
claims.quote: Due to its highly adsorptive and corrosive nature, HF is often underestimated, or entirely lost, in unheated or metallic transfer lines. As a result, existing safety models and hazard assessments may be based on HF levels that are orders of magnitude lower than the true concentrations.

claims.claim: LIBs with lithium-nickel-cobalt-aluminium (NCA) cathode chemistry had a laminar burning velocity range between 0.2 m/s to 1.1 m/s, which is higher than typical hydrocarbons.
claims.quote: LIBs with lithium-nickel-cobalt-aluminium (NCA) cathode chemistry had a higher LBV than typical hydrocarbons, with an LBV range between 0.2 m/s to 1.1 m/s.

claims.claim: DNV reported an explosion pressure between 90 kPa (gauge) and 270 kPa (gauge) in a partly confined geometry with various Li-ion vent gas release profiles.
claims.quote: DNV reported an explosion pressure between 90 kPa (gauge) and 270 kPa (gauge) in a partly confined geometry, with various Li-ion vent gas release profiles.

claims.claim: The maximum explosion pressure in the 1-m channel experiments was 1.36 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio of 1.08, while the CFD simulation predicted 1.45 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio of 1.0.
claims.quote: the maximum explosion pressure was 1.36 kPa (gauge), at φ equal to 1.08; whereas, the maximum explosion pressure for the simulation was 1.45 kPa (gauge), at φ equal to 1.0.

claims.claim: At 650 K, the Gülder equation underpredicts LBVs on the lean and rich side with a standard deviation of error of 338 mm/s and R² of 0.66.
claims.quote: At 650 K, the SDE is 338 mm/s, and R2 is 0.66.

claims.claim: Pressure and flame front velocity oscillations that occurred as the flame approached the channel opening were not accurately predicted by the CFD simulations.
claims.quote: pressure and flame front velocity oscillations, which occurred as the flame approached the channel opening, were not accurately predicted.

claims.claim: In all three chemistries (NCA, LCO, LMO), ignition time decreased with increasing SOC, indicating lower thermal stability of electroactive materials at higher SOC.
claims.quote: all chemistries showed a decrease in ignition time with the increase in SOC, indicating lower thermal stability of electroactive materials at higher SOC.

claims.claim: TRonset decreased with increasing SOC in all three chemistries (NCA, LCO, LMO), with NCA decreasing from 171.5°C to 146.9°C, LCO from 173.9°C to 142.3°C, and LMO from 192.5°C across 25% to 100% SOC.
claims.quote: it is apparent that in all chemistries, TRonset decreased with the increase in SOC. For instance, from 25% to 100% SOC. TRonset decreased from 171.5°C to 146.9°C, and 192.5°C for NCA, LCO, and LMO LIBs, respectively.

claims.claim: Among the three chemistries tested, LMO showed the lowest TRonset values at all SOC levels, which is associated with its quicker failure and shorter burning time.
claims.quote: Among the three chemistries, LMO showed the lowest TRonset values in all SOCs. This justifies its quicker failure and shorter burning time, as depicted in Fig. S2c.

claims.claim: Safety vent crack in LCO occurred when battery surface temperature reached between 155°C and 185°C, in NCA between 135°C and 165°C, and in LMO between 145°C and 175°C.
claims.quote: the safety vent crack was observed to occur when battery surface temperatures reached between 155°C - 185°C; 135°C - 165°C; and 145°C - 175°C for LCO, NCA, and LMO, respectively.

claims.claim: The peak flame temperature at 20 mm above the LIB is larger than typical LIB gaps in arrays or modules, implying neighboring LIBs may be ignited in this type of burning.
claims.quote: The temperature at the top of LIB (20 mm) was the highest in relation to the other positions. At this position the thermocouple was mainly covered by the hottest flame region. This distance (20 mm) is larger than typical LIB gaps in arrays or modules, and so neighboring LIBs may be inflamed in this type of burning.

claims.claim: The empirical equations for predicting TRonset achieved a percentage error of less than 10% for both NCA and LCO LIBs, with the largest discrepancy at lower SOC (maximum %Er of 7.4% for NCA and 2.7% for LCO at 20% SOC).
claims.quote: The %Er ranges from 0.6% to 7.4% and is independent of the SOC. The largest disparity is seen in the lower SOC, with maximum %Er of 2.7 and 7.4% in LCO and NCA, respectively.

claims.claim: The empirical equations for TRonset, Tsurf, and Tflame are applicable only for NCA and LCO cathode chemistries with 18650 form factors externally heated 2 cm from the heater in an open chamber with no forced air, and are not applicable to LMO due to violent explosions.
claims.quote: the empirical equations developed are found to be applicable for NCA and LCO cathodic chemistries with 18650 form factors externally heated 2 cm from the heater in an open chamber with no forced air... LMO is not taken into account because of the violent explosions observed in many of its samples, which could cause a large discrepancy in the prediction.

claims.claim: FSN during venting increased with SOC up to 100% SOC in NCA and LCO, but during explosion the FSN uptrend reversed after 50% SOC due to violent explosion effects.
claims.quote: During the venting process, the FSN in NCA and LCO LIBs increased with SOC up to 100% SOC, indicating that SOC has an effect on FSN. However, during the explosion, the FSN showed an uptrend up to 50% SOC, after which the FSN values were lowered by the violent explosion.

claims.claim: LCO with 100% SOC stored 9.01 Wh and did not burst the canister, whereas LMO with 100% SOC stored 12.34 Wh and did burst the canister, demonstrating that energy content determines canister rupture behavior across chemistries.
claims.quote: LCO with 100% SOC has 9.01 Wh and did not burst the canister, but LMO with 100% SOC has 12.34 Wh and did break the canister.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway was not observed when the LIB was aligned vertically with the anode terminal facing the horizontally aligned heater, because only a small surface was heated and the received thermal stress was insufficient to trigger TR.
claims.quote: TR was not observed when the LIB under test was aligned vertically with anode terminal facing the horizontally aligned heater. The major reason is that only a small LIB's surface was heated, and the received thermal stress was not enough to trigger TR.

claims.claim: EMC has a higher maximum explosion pressure (0.62 MPa) and rate of pressure rise (35.90 MPa/s) compared to DMC (0.59 MPa and 27.50 MPa/s) and BVG (0.45 MPa and 25.20 MPa/s), measured at 393 K and 0.10 MPa initial conditions in an 8 L cylindrical vessel.
claims.quote: The PMax and (dp/dt)Max of EMC are higher than those of DMC and BVG. For EMC, they are are 0.62 MPa and 35.8 MPa/s, respectively. For DMC, they are 0.59 MPa and 27.50 MPa/s, respectively. For BVG, they are 0.45 MPa and 25.20 MPa/s, respectively.

claims.claim: The flammability limits for EMC, DMC, and BVG were determined to be 2.35%–14.65%, 3.50%–17.00%, and 10.20%–47.50%, respectively, at 393 K.
claims.quote: The flammability limits for EMC, DMC, and BVG were determined to be 2.35 % to 14.65 %, 3.50 % to 17.00 %, and 10.20 % to 47.50 %, respectively.

claims.claim: As carbonate mixture content increases in mixtures with BVG, the LFL decreases while maximum overpressure, maximum rate of pressure rise, and LOC all increase.
claims.quote: The addition of carbonate mixtures to BVG results in a decrease in the LFL value, while PMax, (dp/dt)Max and LOC values all increase.

claims.claim: As temperature rises from 298 K to 393 K, the LOC of BVG decreases from 7.39% to 7.03%.
claims.quote: The results indicate that the LOC of BVG gradually decreases with increasing temperature. As the temperature rises from 298 K to 393 K, the LOC of BVG decreases from 7.39 % to 7.03 %.

claims.claim: When carbonate mixture content increases in BVG mixtures, PMax increases from 0.45 MPa to 0.61 MPa, (dp/dt)Max increases from 25.20 MPa/s to 35.50 MPa/s, and KG increases from 5.00 to 7.10 (MPa·m)/s, with LOC increasing from 7.03% to 10.80%.
claims.quote: As carbonate mixture contents increases, the PMax of BVG increases from 0.45 MPa to 0.61 MPa, and (dp/dt)Max increases from 25.20 MPa/s to 35.50 MPa/s, and KG increases from 5.00 (MPa⋅m)/s to 7.10 (MPa⋅m)/s... the LOC of mixtures of carbonates and BVG increased from 7.03 % to 10.80 %.

claims.claim: The LEL of vented gas from LIB thermal runaway increases in the initial stage and then declines with increasing SOC, with the highest LEL occurring at 60% SOC.
claims.quote: the LEL of the vented gas increases in initial stage and then declines with the increase of the batteries SOCs... the highest LEL is at 60% SOC.

claims.claim: At low SOC, less violent thermal runaway allows battery electrolyte to react with air more adequately, producing more CO2, which raises the LEL as a non-flammable gas.
claims.quote: In initial stage, the LEL increases when the SOC is at a low level, which is the result of less violent thermal runaway and battery electrolyte can react with the air adequately. This combustion reaction produces a large amount of carbon dioxide (CO2), which makes the LEL higher as a kind of non-flammable gas.

claims.claim: At high SOC, more violent thermal runaway produces more CO because internal materials cannot react with air timely and adequately, which lowers the LEL as CO is a flammable gas.
claims.quote: The thermal runaway takes place dramatically when it comes to a high SOC stage, while the internal materials in the battery cannot react with the air timely and adequately, producing much CO. The CO makes the LEL lower as a kind of flammable gas.

claims.claim: The LEL of vented gas declines with increasing heating temperature, with higher temperature producing more violent thermal runaway and a higher proportion of flammable gas.
claims.quote: the LEL of the vented gas declines with the increase of heating temperature in general. The higher temperature when the thermal runaway happens makes the runaway more violent, and the proportion of flammable gas is higher.

claims.claim: The LEL of vented gas declines with increasing heating power, with the lowest LEL recorded in this work being 4.80%, which can guide design of explosion-proof apparatus and alarm settings for flammable gas detectors.
claims.quote: the LEL of the vented gas declines with the increase of heating power obviously... The lowest LEL is 4.80% in this work, which can provide guidance for design of explosion-proof electric apparatus and setting alarm parameter of flammable gas detectors in a LIB container or warehouse.

claims.claim: When the ESU door was opened, decreased residual Novec 1230 concentration and suddenly increased oxygen concentration combined with runaway-generated gases to form a flammable mixture, which was ignited producing a deflagration.
claims.quote: The resulting decreased residual Novec 1230 concentration and suddenly increased oxygen concentration, combined with runaway generated gases, formed a flammable mixture in a portion of the enclosure.

claims.claim: The Novec 1230 suppression system discharged as designed but was inherently unable to curtail the progression of cell thermal runaways, and the room integrity tests indicated leakage rate was too large to retain the design concentration for 10 minutes as required by NFPA 2001.
claims.quote: the system was inherently unable to curtail the progression of cell thermal runaways in Rack 15...the room integrity tests conducted upon commissioning of the Novec 1230 system indicated that the leakage rate was too large to retain the design concentration for 10 min, as required by NFPA 2001, 2015

claims.claim: Comparison of calculated and actual vent areas suggests the flammable mixture volume occupied 11%–22% of the enclosure volume at the time of the APS explosion, with a laminar burning velocity in the range 0.18–0.22 m/s.
claims.quote: the results in Table 2 suggest that the flammable gas mixture likely occupied between 11% and 22% of the enclosure volume, and probably was at an equivalence ratio in the range 1.6–1.8

claims.claim: Calculated arc flash pressures for rack enclosures are an order-of-magnitude larger than those for module enclosures and are higher than the enclosures can withstand, though actual pressures are significantly lower when enclosures are vented.
claims.quote: The calculated arc flash pressures for rack enclosures are an order-of-magnitude larger than those for module enclosures, and are higher than the enclosures can withstand. However, these enclosures usually are vented, which make actual arc pressures significantly lower than the values shown in Table 3.

claims.claim: A cell temperature rise of at least 64°C produced a cell temperature exceeding the 80°C threshold for onset of cell membrane deterioration and exothermic decomposition reactions.
claims.quote: The corresponding recorded cell temperature rise was at least 64°C, producing a cell temperature in excess of the 80°C threshold for the onset of cell membrane deterioration and exothermic decomposition reactions

claims.claim: The maximum HRR values were 5.04, 4.26, and 2.31 kW at 100%, 50%, and 0% SOC respectively, with maximum HRR increasing significantly as SOC increased.
claims.quote: The maximum HRRs were 5.04, 4.26, and 2.31 kW at 100, 50, and 0% SOC respectively; the maximum HRR increased significantly as SOC increased

claims.claim: An explosion occurred during the 2nd combustion stage for 50% and 100% SOC batteries, but no explosion occurred for the 0% SOC battery.
claims.quote: An explosion occurred at the 2nd combustion stage for 50% and 100% SOCs batteries but no explosion for 0% SOC.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the second combustion was observed at 520 s, compared to 275 s at 100% SOC and 361 s at 50% SOC, and was both delayed and lacking an explosion.
claims.quote: At 0% SOC, the first combustion was observed at 280 s, followed by the second combustion at 520 s as the smoke ignited. Compared to the 100% SOC and 50% SOC fires, the second combustion differed greatly, being both delayed and lacking an explosion.

claims.claim: The CO/CO2 ratio in the 2nd combustion stage is higher than in the 1st combustion stage, due to small flow time (or mixing time) in the process of conversion of CO to CO2 in the partially premixed flame.
claims.quote: the CO/CO2 ratio in the 2nd combustion stage is higher than in the 1st combustion stage. Considering that the 1st combustion stage is characterized by a diffusion flame and the 2nd combustion stage is by a partially premixed flame, it can be confirmed that the relatively high concentration of CO is due to the small flow time (or mixing time) in the process of conversion to CO2.

claims.claim: Particle concentration was detected only in the 1st combustion stage and was hardly detected at 100% and 50% SOC in the 2nd combustion stage, confirming the 2nd stage as a characteristic of a partially premixed flame.
claims.quote: it is found that the particle density is hardly detected at 100% and 50% SOC in the 2nd combustion stage, confirming that it is a characteristic of a partially premixed flame.

claims.claim: Battery content temperature exceeded 800°C during impact-induced thermal runaway, as recorded by thermography.
claims.quote: Thermography of the aftermath showed that the battery content reached a temperature higher than 800°C, which is higher than simulated value in (Md Said and Mohd Tohir, 2019).

claims.claim: Flame temperature during impact-induced thermal runaway can exceed 500°C, as shown by thermography.
claims.quote: The second hazard is the flame erupted due to the ignition of flammable electrolytes and combustible gases produced by battery components. As shown by thermography, the flame temperature can be higher than 500°C.

claims.claim: In a 3x3 battery module test, thermal runaway in the impacted cell did not propagate to neighbouring cells, which remained at room temperature after impact.
claims.quote: Even though violent failure behaviour was observed from the impact test, the large amount of heat was unable to induce thermal runaway reactions in the neighbouring cells. Thermography showed that all the surrounding cells remained at room temperature after the impact.

claims.claim: Limited contact surface area between cylindrical cells retards heat transfer and prevents thermal runaway propagation to neighbouring cells.
claims.quote: Limited contact surface area retards the heat transfer process and deters the propagation of thermal runaway to the neighbouring cells.

claims.claim: In the first impact test, thermocouple temperature at interstitial spaces rose from room temperature to 34°C, plateaued for 9 seconds, then surged to 250°C before dropping after completion of thermal runaway reactions.
claims.quote: Upon indented by the steel rod, the temperature as recorded by thermocouples increased slightly from room temperature to 34°C and appeared to plateau for 9 s before surging to 250°C. The temperature dropped after completion of thermal runaway reactions.

claims.claim: At high SOC, lithium ion cells tend to fail via flaming, overpressure, and flaring, which can be a forceful event with torch-like flames involving large amounts of flammable gas.
claims.quote: At a high SOC, lithium ion cells tend to fail via flaming, overpressure, and flaring. This can be a forceful event with torch-like flames, which involves a large amount of flammable gas.

claims.claim: Non-flaming venting tends to occur at a lower SOC, consisting of gas or aerosol ejected through overpressure venting.
claims.quote: Non-flaming venting tends to occur at a lower SOC. The failure event will consist of an amount of gas or aerosol ejected through overpressure venting.

claims.claim: The mean Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) of lithium ion cell vent gas was determined to be on the order of 10%, based on 21 cell varieties tested at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: Archibald and EPRI (2021) determined lithium ion cell vent gas statistical properties for 21 varieties and concluded that the mean Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) was on the order of 10%. It shall be said that Archibald used cells at 100% SOC.

claims.claim: In a 40-ft ISO standard container BESS, a single 94-Ah cell provides enough flammable gas during thermal runaway for a partial volume deflagration to occur.
claims.quote: EPRI established in a recent BESS explosion hazards technical bulletin (EPRI, 2021) that in a 40-ft ISO standard container BESS, a single 94-Ah cell would provide enough flammable gas during thermal runaway for a partial volume deflagration to occur.

claims.claim: HF gas production is directly proportional to the electrical energy stored in the cell or battery and can be conservatively estimated at 200 mg of HF per Wh.
claims.quote: The HF gas production is directly proportional to the electrical energy stored in the cell or battery and can be conservatively estimated with 200 mg of HF/ Wh (Larsson et al., 2017).

claims.claim: A thermal runaway event with fire lowers the probability of large and catastrophic explosions because flammable gases liberated during propagating thermal runaway are partially consumed in the fire.
claims.quote: a thermal runaway with fire (i.e., a burning reaction) lowers the probability of large and catastrophic explosions. This fact should be considered when considering the implementation alternative suppression systems such as clean agents.

claims.claim: Clean agent suppression systems can extinguish flames but may indirectly contribute to the accumulation of flammable gases inside the enclosure, increasing explosion risk.
claims.quote: Those systems can be effective in extinguishing flaming (Wang et al., 2016), but they may indirectly contribute to the accumulation of flammable gases inside the enclosure.

claims.claim: HF has the lowest IDLH value among the commonly reported toxic gases from lithium-ion battery failures, with an IDLH of 30 ppm, compared to CO at 1200 ppm, SO2 at 100 ppm, NO at 100 ppm, HCl at 50 ppm, and HCN at 50 ppm.
claims.quote: In terms of IDLH, HF is the most dangerous gas out of the ones listed above, with an IDLH value of 30 ppm... Both HCN and HCl have an IDLH value of 50 ppm... Both SO2 and NO have an IDLH value of 100 ppm, and CO has an IDLH value of 1200 ppm.

claims.claim: In burning battery failure simulations, the most dangerous phase in terms of downwind toxicity did not occur at peak mass flow rate but at approximately 1 kg/s, because above 1 kg/s increased HRR raises plume buoyancy and reduces safety distance more than the increase in toxic gas mass flow rate extends it.
claims.quote: for the burning case, the 'worst-case' timestep corresponded to a mass flow rate of approximately 1 kg/s rather than to the peak. This means that the most dangerous phase (in terms of downwind toxicity) of a battery fire may not occur at its peak... When the total mass flow rate is smaller than 1 kg/s, the second effect dominates, and the safety distance increases with the mass flow rate. However, beyond 1 kg/s, the first effect dominates, and the safety distance drops with the total mass flow rate.

claims.claim: For non-burning battery failure simulations, safety distance did not monotonically increase with wind speed; the peak safety distance occurred at 8 km/h (5 mph), with higher wind speeds reducing safety distance due to increased dilution.
claims.quote: The main difference with the burning cases is that safety distance did not monotonically increase with wind speed. For the 100% SOC cases, Fig. 15 shows safety distance as a function of wind speed for all battery energies. The peak safety distance occurs at 8 km/h (5 mph) for all cases. Up to 8 km/h (5 mph), the increasing wind speeds increase advection... However, as the wind speed increases further, the trend is reversed. The increasing wind speeds increase dilution, which reduces the safety distances.

claims.claim: For burning battery failure simulations, the variation in toxic gas yields was the most impactful parameter on safety distance, with higher yields producing greater safety distances, and HF yield was the dominant factor because HF set the safety distance in all burning simulation cases.
claims.quote: For the burning case, the most impactful parameters were the toxic gas yields. Unsurprisingly, the higher the yields are, the greater the safety distance is... For all burning simulations, HF was the gas setting the safety distance. This means that HF was the gas that required the largest distance to drop below its IDLH.

claims.claim: EV and ICEV fires simulated using RISE experimental data produced similar safety distances of approximately 4–5 m, but the EV produced substantially more HF, resulting in much higher HF concentrations downwind compared to the ICEV.
claims.quote: There were no significant differences in terms of safety distance between the two simulations... For both simulations, the maximum safety distance was around 4–5 m... For the ICEV simulation, the HF concentration did not reach IDLH. The HF plume for the EV simulation extended outside of the room.

claims.claim: The maximum peak overpressure (PMax) of DMC is 31% higher than that of BVG, measured at 393 K and 0.1 MPa in an 8 L cylindrical vessel.
claims.quote: The maximum Pex of DMC is 31% higher than that of BVG. This is mainly due to the presence of inert gases in BVG, which can suppress explosions, and DMC has higher combustion heat than BVG

claims.claim: As xDMC increased from 0.1 to 0.5, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 12.2% and 17.7% respectively, while increasing xDMC from 0.6 to 0.9 yielded only 5.5% and 5.6% increases respectively.
claims.quote: When xDMC increased from 0.1 to 0.5, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 12.2% and 17.7%, respectively. However, PMax and (dp/dt)Max increased by 5.5% and 5.6%, respectively, during the increment of xDMC from 0.6 to 0.9.

claims.claim: The LFL of DMC/BVG mixtures decreases approximately linearly as initial temperature increases from 363 K to 423 K, with larger xDMC values showing greater LFL reduction over this temperature range.
claims.quote: When the initial temperature was increased from 363 K to 423 K, LFL, corresponding to xDMC = 0.1, xDMC = 0.5, and xDMC = 0.9, decreased by 4.85%, 9.73%, and 12.82%, respectively.

claims.claim: The LOC of DMC/BVG mixtures increases linearly with increasing xDMC (R² = 0.97) because 1 mol DMC consumes approximately 5.5 times more oxygen than 1 mol BVG for complete combustion.
claims.quote: As xDMC increases, the LOC of the mixture shows a linear increase trend (R2 = 0.97)... the oxygen consumption of the DMC is about 5.5 times that of the BVG, which explains the phenomenon of the mixture LOC increasing with the increase of DMC fraction.

claims.claim: When xDMC is less than 0.3, LOC is lower than LFL and combustible concentration is the priority safety concern; when xDMC exceeds 0.3, LOC is higher than LFL and oxygen concentration becomes the preferred control parameter.
claims.quote: the LOC is lower than the LFL until xDMC = 0.3, and the combustibles' concentration should be give priority to in the system. When xDMC>0.3, the LOC is higher than the LFL and the O2 concentration is preferred to be considered.

claims.claim: At equivalence ratio φ = 0.5, N2 concentration needs to exceed 50% to completely inhibit BVG explosion, while CO2 only needs to exceed 30%.
claims.quote: When φ = 0.5, the concentration of N2 needs to be more than 50% to completely inhibit the explosion of the BVG reaction, while CO2 only needs to be more than 30%.

claims.claim: At equivalence ratio φ = 0.8, increasing CO2 concentration from 5% to 25% reduced Pmax from 0.53 MPa to 0.28 MPa (a 47.17% reduction), reduced (dp/dt)max from 21 MPa/s to 1.8 MPa/s (a 91.43% reduction), and increased tex from 83 ms to 570 ms (a 586.75% increase).
claims.quote: The concentration of CO2 increased from 5% to 25%, at which time the Pmax was reduced from 0.53 MPa to 0.28 MPa, a reduction of 47.17%; (dp/dt)max was reduced from 21 MPa/s to 1.8 MPa/s, a reduction of 91.43%; tex increased from 83 ms to 570 ms, with an increase of 586.75%.

claims.claim: Under jet conditions at φ = 0.5, the presence of an air jet increased Pex from 0.1 MPa to 0.37 MPa, increased dp/dt from 0.35 MPa/s to 8.2 MPa/s, and decreased tex from 746 ms to 135 ms.
claims.quote: When φ = 0.5, the jet condition has a greater enhancement of the explosion hazard of BVG. Pex increased from 0.1 MPa to 0.37 MPa, dp/dt increased from 0.35 MPa/s to 8.2 MPa/s, and tex decreased from 746 ms to 135 ms in the presence of air jet.

claims.claim: Under premixed conditions at φ = 0.5, a CO2 volume concentration exceeding 30% completely inhibits BVG explosion, but under jet conditions the CO2 concentration must exceed 35% to achieve complete inhibition.
claims.quote: Under premixed conditions, a volume concentration of CO2 of more than 30% could completely inhibit the explosion of BVG. However, under the jet effect, the concentration of CO2 needs to exceed 35% to completely inhibit the explosion of BVG.

claims.claim: At φ = 0.6 with 100 g/m3 graphite dust, the inhibition effect of CO2 on hybrid explosion (maximum reduced Pex of 0.28 MPa, ratio 46.7%) is better than the inhibition effect of CO2 jet on BVG alone (maximum reduced Pex of 0.12 MPa, ratio 27.3%), because unburned graphite dust absorbs heat and CO2 reduces oxygen availability for graphite combustion.
claims.quote: the inhibition effect of CO2 on the explosion of hybrids is better than the inhibition effect of CO2 jet on the explosion of BVG. This is because in addition to the inert mechanism of CO2 on BVG, a large amount of unburned graphite is produced. These unburned graphite dust also absorb the heat of the BVG explosion.

claims.claim: For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions.
claims.quote: For NCA cells, the TR phase exhibited the most critical BVG composition, while for LFP and NMC cells, the venting phase proved more critical, largely due to H2 emissions.

claims.claim: Su values increase with higher state of charge (SoC) for battery vent gases.
claims.quote: Baird et al. (2020) observed that Su values increase with higher SoC

claims.claim: NCA cells exhibited a maximum Su of 1.0 m/s, higher than LCO cells (0.6 m/s) and LFP cells (0.3 m/s), attributable to higher hydrogen and lower carbon dioxide content in NCA vent gas.
claims.quote: Henriksen et al. (2021b) reported that NCA cells exhibited a maximum Su (1.0 m/s), higher than that of LCO (0.6 m/s) and LFP cells (0.3 m/s). This behaviour is largely attributable to the BVG composition: NCA cells release gases with higher hydrogen and lower carbon dioxide content compared to other cells.

claims.claim: The peak of laminar burning velocity occurs at an equivalent ratio of around 1.0 across all cell types and SoC levels.
claims.quote: the peak of Su occurs at an equivalent ratio (ER) of around 1.0 across all cell types and SoC levels.

claims.claim: Increasing temperature significantly enhances Su across all BVG compositions, with LFP cells attaining a maximum Su of 735 cm/s at 500°C in the TR phase, NMC cells reaching 574 cm/s, and NCA cells peaking at 345 cm/s for the global BVG at 500°C.
claims.quote: Fig. 7 demonstrates that increasing temperature significantly enhances Su across all BVG compositions, with notable differences between chemistries. NCA cells show relatively consistent Su values across BVG compositions, peaking at 345 cm/s for the global BVG at 500°C. In contrast, LFP cells attain a higher maximum Su (735 cm/s, at 500°C) in the TR phase, while NMC cells reach an intermediate Su (574 cm/s) in the same phase.

claims.claim: For NMC cells, the venting phase with high H2 content yields a significantly higher Su (222.6 cm/s) than the TR phase (77.2 cm/s) and global event (59.9 cm/s), with the decline in Su in later phases aligning with increasing CO2 and decreasing H2 percentages.
claims.quote: the venting phase, with its high H2 content, yields significantly higher Su values (222.6 cm/s) than the TR phase (77.2 cm/s) and global event (59.9 cm/s). This decline in Su in later phases aligns with the increase in CO2 and decrease in H2 percentages as the thermal abuse test progresses.

claims.claim: For LFP cells, the TR BVG composition yields the highest Su (105.9 cm/s), followed by the venting BVG (88.2 cm/s) and the global BVG (42.0 cm/s), with the higher Su in the TR phase driven by H2 released at TR onset, while the subsequent increase in CO2 mole fraction tempers the global Su.
claims.quote: the TR BVG composition yields the highest Su (105.9 cm/s), followed by the venting BVG (88.2 cm/s) and the global BVG (42.0 cm/s). These variations are found to be correlated with the H2 mole fraction, as this species is released at the time of TR onset, driving the significantly higher Su in the TR phase. The subsequent increase in CO2 mole fraction from TR onset to its peak tempers the global Su.

claims.claim: For NCA cells at standard conditions, the highest Su value (45.5 cm/s) occurs for the global BVG composition, followed by 44.4 cm/s in the TR phase and 32.5 cm/s in the venting phase, with differences arising from variations in H2 mole fractions across phases.
claims.quote: The results show that the highest Su value (45.5 cm/s) occurs when considering the global composition over the entire event, followed by 44.4 cm/s in the TR phase and 32.5 cm/s in the venting phase. These differences arise from variations in the H2 mole fractions across phases, with higher H2 concentration during the TR and global phases leading to elevated Su values compared to the venting phase.

claims.claim: The maximum temperatures reached during thermal runaway were 562 ± 96°C for NCA, 358 ± 70°C for LFP, and 575 ± 101°C for NMC cells, with NCA and NMC cells reaching maximum pressures surpassing 4 barg and LFP cells showing a lower peak pressure under 2 barg.
